title: Declarative UI and re-rendering

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific React: Component-Based Browser Applications draft for Declarative UI and re-rendering; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: React Learn and Reference; React 19.2; provisions applicable to Declarative UI and re-rendering
sources.claim: React components describe UI as a function of props, state, and context, and reconciliation updates the DOM to match the latest description.

canonicalExplanation.summary: React components describe UI as a function of props, state, and context, and reconciliation updates the DOM to match the latest description.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Changing isOpen causes render to describe either the panel or its absence without manually inserting nodes. Your goal is to translate a manual DOM update into state-driven rendering and trace its rerender. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: javascript project browser app; Application root and rendering; javascript dom query update. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: React components describe UI as a function of props, state, and context, and reconciliation updates the DOM to match the latest description. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Render must stay pure, browser state can exist outside React, and imperative escape hatches need synchronization. This draft therefore rejects: Declarative React means DOM events and state updates never occur imperatively.

includes: Describe UI as a function of current props and state rather than imperative DOM mutations.
Translate a manual DOM update into state-driven rendering and trace its rerender.
excludes: Render must stay pure, browser state can exist outside React, and imperative escape hatches need synchronization.

outcomes: Translate a manual DOM update into state-driven rendering and trace its rerender.

examples.title: Declarative UI and re-rendering: concrete case
examples.situation: Changing isOpen causes render to describe either the panel or its absence without manually inserting nodes.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: Changing isOpen causes render to describe either the panel or its absence without manually inserting nodes.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Describe UI from state and use event handlers to update state; reserve DOM imperative work for explicit escape hatches.
examples.result: The result follows because Describe UI from state and use event handlers to update state; reserve DOM imperative work for explicit escape hatches.

examples.title: Declarative UI and re-rendering: boundary case
examples.situation: Render must stay pure, browser state can exist outside React, and imperative escape hatches need synchronization.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Render must stay pure, browser state can exist outside React, and imperative escape hatches need synchronization.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Declarative React means DOM events and state updates never occur imperatively.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Describe UI from state and use event handlers to update state; reserve DOM imperative work for explicit escape hatches.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Declarative React means DOM events and state updates never occur imperatively.
misconceptions.correction: Describe UI from state and use event handlers to update state; reserve DOM imperative work for explicit escape hatches.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use a concrete React: Component-Based Browser Applications artifact to explain why this belief fails: Declarative React means DOM events and state updates never occur imperatively.

analogy.example: A state-driven component workshop can model Declarative UI and re-rendering, where inputs and state produce a UI description.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Declarative UI and re-rendering before using exact platform terms
limitations: A workshop does not reproduce reconciliation identity, hook ordering, concurrent rendering, effects, or browser accessibility APIs.

recap.summary: You should now be able to translate a manual DOM update into state-driven rendering and trace its rerender. Keep this boundary in view: Render must stay pure, browser state can exist outside React, and imperative escape hatches need synchronization.
